scenario_description: A user documents the renovation of a kitchen, tracking a specific load-bearing beam through three stages: painted white with a label, stripped and surrounded by wires, and finally covered in drywall. The final turn requires identifying the original beam among three similar supports based on subtle structural details visible across the sequence.

turns.text: Here's the wall before we started. That big white support is labeled 'Beam A'.
turns.image_description: A wide shot of a kitchen wall during early demolition. A large, horizontal wooden beam runs across the center, painted bright white. On the right side of this beam, a handwritten label 'Beam A' is taped to the surface. To the left of Beam A, there is a smaller, unpainted vertical stud. To the far right, partially cut off by the frame, is another thick wooden beam that looks identical in size but has no paint and no label. The lighting is bright and even.

turns.text: Got it. I see the white beam with the 'Beam A' tag clearly. Is that the one you're worried about removing?
Yeah, we had to strip the paint to get to the wiring. It looks pretty messy now.
turns.image_description: The same horizontal beam from Turn 1 is now bare wood, the white paint completely gone. The 'Beam A' label is missing. Several thick black electrical wires are draped over and around the beam, obscuring parts of its surface. The beam shows a distinct, dark knot near the center-right section where the label used to be. The smaller vertical stud to the left is still visible, unchanged. The thick beam on the far right (seen in Turn 1) is now fully visible; it is also bare wood but has no wires on it and features a clean grain pattern with no knots in the corresponding area.

turns.text: That definitely looks rougher without the paint. Those wires make it hard to tell exactly where the old markings were, though.
Finally got the drywall up. Can you spot which one is the original support?
turns.image_description:
The wall is now covered in fresh, light gray drywall. Three horizontal wooden supports protrude slightly from the drywall at different heights or positions, all looking like raw timber beams. 
Option A (Left): A beam with a small, round knot near the center.
Option B (Center): A beam that is perfectly straight with smooth grain and no visible knots.
Option C (Right): A beam with a large, dark, irregular knot near the right side, roughly matching the position of the knot seen in Turn 3. The drywall around Option C has slight imperfections suggesting recent work.

turns.text: They all look very similar once they're behind the drywall. Without seeing the original grain patterns or labels, it's tough to say for sure which one matches the history.

Looking at the three exposed ends in this photo, which one corresponds to the original 'Beam A'? A) The left one with the small central knot. B) The center one with the smooth grain. C) The right one with the large dark knot on the right side.

ground_truth.reasoning_chain: 1. In Turn 1, 'Beam A' is identified as the central white beam. 2. In Turn 3, after paint removal, 'Beam A' is revealed to have a distinct dark knot near the center-right section. Crucially, the other similar beam (originally on the far right in Turn 1) is shown to have no such knot. 3. In Turn 5, three options are presented. Option A has a small central knot (mismatch). Option B has no knot (mismatch). Option C has a large dark knot on the right side, matching the unique identifier established in Turn 3. Therefore, Option C is the correct entity.
ground_truth.key_difficulty: The model must track a specific visual feature (the location and shape of a wood knot) that appears only in the middle turn (Turn 3) when the paint is removed. This feature is not present in the first turn (obscured by paint/label) or explicitly described in text. The distractors in the final turn rely on plausible but incorrect knot placements that would fool a model relying only on the final image or general knowledge of wood grain.

_meta.scenario: A user documents the demolition of a kitchen wall, showing a specific load-bearing beam initially painted white and labeled 'Beam A'. In subsequent turns, the beam is stripped of paint, surrounded by exposed wiring, and finally covered in fresh drywall, with the label removed. The final turn asks the user to identify which structural element corresponds to the original 'Beam A' among three similar-looking wooden supports.
